coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution on graphene quantum dots anchored TiO2 nanotubes-array
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Hydrogen Energy Publications, LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications, LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Yifu
Ren, Jiale
Meng, Ming
coredata.dc:description: The inversely structured TiO2 nanotubes-array (TiO2-NA) and CdS-modified TiO2 nanotubes-array (CdS/TiO2-NA) with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) anchored inside were prepared through a facile impregnation method. The catalysts were characterized by multiple techniques of SEM, TEM, XRD, Raman spectroscopy, XPS, TG and diffuse reflectance UV/Vis absorption spectroscopy. The results of TEM, XPS and Raman spectroscopy indicate that the GQDs were really formed and successfully anchored into the TiO2-NA and CdS/TiO2-NA. The activity evaluation results show that the hydrogen evolution rate during photocatalytic water splitting was greatly improved after loading GQDs into TiO2-NA and CdS/TiO2-NA. By breaking graphene into GQDs, the light-filtering effect of graphene was remarkably inhibited as compared with that of conventional large graphene sheets. Moreover, the overall morphology of TiO2 nanotube array could be well maintained after anchoring GQDs inside, which is favorable to mass transfer. The catalyst design strategy proposed in present work can be extended to other photocatalytic systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Inverse structure
Graphene quantum dots
Water splitting
TiO2 nanotubes-array

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Graphene Oxide Quantum Dots (GOQDs) and Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs)
Preparation of self-organized TiO2 nanotubes array (TiO2-NA)
Preparation of CdS loaded TiO2 nanotubes array (CdS/TiO2-NA)
Preparation of GQD loaded TiO2 nanotubes array (GQD/TiO2-NA)
Preparation of GQD and CdS loaded TiO2 nanotubes array (GQD/CdS/TiO2-NA)
Preparation of graphene loaded TiO2 nanotubes array (GE/TiO2-NA)
Preparation of graphene and CdS loaded TiO2 nanotubes array (GE/CdS/TiO2-NA)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the GOQDs and (b) their size distributions; (c) the excitation-dependent photoluminescence spectra of GOQDs (dissolved in water) as the GOQDs were excited at the wavelength from 320 to 550 nm with an interval of 20 nm.

(a) A typical top-view SEM image and (b) cross-sectional view of the well aligned TiO2-NA film. Inset in (a): high magnification SEM image for (a). Inset in (b): the bottom view of the well aligned TiO2-NA film; (c) TEM and (d) HR-TEM images of CdS/TiO2-NA film. Inset in (c): EDX spectrum of CdS/TiO2-NA corresponding to the red circle area in (c); (e) size distribution for CdS quantum dots. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Photoluminescence spectra and (b) diffuse reflectance UV/Vis absorption spectra for GOQDs and GQDs.
Diffuse reflectance UV/Vis absorption spectra of the as-prepared catalysts.
C1s binding energy spectra for the samples: (a) GOQD/CdS/TiO2-NA and (b) GQD/CdS/TiO2-NA.

(a) SEM image, EDX spectrum corresponding to the green rectangular area and the atomic percentages calculated from EDX results of sample CdS/TiO2-NA; (b) TG curve of sample GQD/TiO2-NA obtained in air with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of hydrogen production efficiency upon the (□) TiO2-NA, (○) GQD/TiO2-NA, (▵) CdS/TiO2-NA, and (☆) GQD/CdS/TiO2-NA photoanodes.
The hydrogen generation rate under different incident light intensity over the catalysts.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution on graphene quantum dots anchored TiO2 nanotubes-array

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Synergic Innovative Center for Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Applied Catalysis Science & Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering & Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Synergic Innovative Center for Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Tianjin Key Laboratory of Applied Catalysis Science & Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The inversely structured TiO2 nanotubes-array (TiO2-NA) and CdS-modified TiO2 nanotubes-array (CdS/TiO2-NA) with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) anchored inside were prepared through a facile impregnation method. The catalysts were characterized by multiple techniques of SEM, TEM, XRD, Raman spectroscopy, XPS, TG and diffuse reflectance UV/Vis absorption spectroscopy. The results of TEM, XPS and Raman spectroscopy indicate that the GQDs were really formed and successfully anchored into the TiO2-NA and CdS/TiO2-NA. The activity evaluation results show that the hydrogen evolution rate during photocatalytic water splitting was greatly improved after loading GQDs into TiO2-NA and CdS/TiO2-NA. By breaking graphene into GQDs, the light-filtering effect of graphene was remarkably inhibited as compared with that of conventional large graphene sheets. Moreover, the overall morphology of TiO2 nanotube array could be well maintained after anchoring GQDs inside, which is favorable to mass transfer. The catalyst design strategy proposed in present work can be extended to other photocatalytic systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar energy harvesting through water splitting on semiconductor photocatalysts has attracted much attention due to the rising energy crisis and environmental issues [1–5]. In the past decades, research efforts for photocatalysts have been mainly focused on the following two aspects: (i) modifying the band-gap [6–8] and (ii) facilitating charge separation and transfer [9,10]. Recently, graphene as a rising star material with unique atomic thickness, excellent electron transfer and charge separation ability [10–12], has shown remarkable enhancement effect on photocurrent and photocatalytic performance when it is composed with other semiconductor materials. However, atomic monolayer graphene can also decrease the number of inducing photons through absorbing ∼2% of incident light [13]. Furthermore, this disadvantage of graphene would be magnified in the systems of photoelectrodes or monolithic photocatalysts, where the graphene exists in the form of multilayer sheets. So, the appropriate loading amount and existing form of graphene in photocatalysts are of great importance for application. It is known that graphene is often used in the form of multilayer sheets with the size of several hundred nanometers to micrometers [10–12]. To weaken the effect of light-filtering and simultaneously maintain the charge separation function of graphene, reasonable design of graphene-modified photocatalysts is particularly required. Herein, we designed and prepared the inversely structured photocatalysts by breaking the graphene sheets into GQDs and anchoring them on semiconductor supports. It is highly expected that the function of graphene on charge separation could get well maintained and the shortcoming of filtering light for traditional graphene could also be overcome.

TiO2 is an excellent photocatalyst with long-term stability, low cost, nontoxicity and strong redox ability. However, it always suffers from the fast recombination of photo-induced electron/hole pairs and the selective response to ultraviolet light [8–10]. Since 1999, the anodization growth of self-organized TiO2 nanotube array (TiO2-NA) aligned on Ti substrate has shown great application potential in the area of defined photoelectrodes for photocatalysis and solar cell systems [14–18].

Herein, we adopted monolithic TiO2-NA as support to constitute the photoelectrode catalysts GQD/TiO2-NA and GQD/CdS/TiO2-NA. It is found that no matter whether the co-catalyst CdS exists or not, the photocatalytic activities for hydrogen production could be remarkably enhanced by loading GQDs onto the catalysts. To reveal the role of GQDs in such inversely structured photocatalysts, we also synthesized the corresponding catalysts of TiO2-NA and CdS/TiO2-NA supported large graphene sheets (GE/TiO2-NA and GE/CdS/TiO2-NA). By comparison, it is found that the as-designed inversely structured photocatalysts containing GQDs show much weaker light-filtering effect than the conventional large graphene sheets, especially at low incident light intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Graphene Oxide Quantum Dots (GOQDs) and Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide (GO) was derived from purified graphite powder by a modified Hummers method [19]. GOQDs were prepared by a reported method [20]. In a typical procedure, GO was dispersed in DMF with the concentrations of 300 mg per 10 mL. The GO/DMF solutions underwent ultrasonication treatment for 30 min. The mixed solution was then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave (with a filling ratio of 80%). The sealed vessel was then maintained at 220 °C for 8 h. The obtained product consisted of brown transparent suspension and black precipitates. The brown transparent suspension was collected for vacuum evaporation treatment at 80 °C to obtain solid GOQDs, which was subsequently re-dissolved into water. The resulted GOQDs aqueous solution was very steady and no change was observed even after reservation for as long as two months. The sample GQDs were obtained through annealing reduction of GOQDs powder in argon at 450 °C for 1 h [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of self-organized TiO2 nanotubes array (TiO2-NA)
ce:section.ce:para: Self-organized TiO2-NA was prepared by a two-step anodization of titanium foil as described in previous work [22]. The first-step anodization was performed under 60 V for 1 h in ethylene glycol solution containing 0.5 wt% NH4F and 3 vol% H2O. The as-formed TiO2 was peeled off by intense ultrasonication in deionized water to expose the Ti substrate. The second-step anodization was carried out in the same conditions as mentioned above for 4 h. The TiO2-NA was obtained after vapor-thermally treated at 450 °C in air for 6 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdS loaded TiO2 nanotubes array (CdS/TiO2-NA)
ce:section.ce:para: CdS nanoparticles were loaded into the crystallized TiO2-NA by sequential chemical bath deposition (S-CBD) method [16]. Typically, the TiO2-NA sample was successively immersed in two different beakers for about 20 s in each beaker. One beaker contained 0.05 M Cd(NO3)2 aqueous solution, another contained 0.05 M Na2S. The sample was rinsed for 10 s every time after bath deposition in one beaker to remove the excess precursor solution. Such an immersion cycle was repeated 10 cycles. Finally, the as-prepared samples were dried at 80 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQD loaded TiO2 nanotubes array (GQD/TiO2-NA)
ce:section.ce:para: The composite of GQD/TiO2-NA was obtained by a simple impregnation method. Firstly, the TiO2-NA was immersed in a 0.01 mg mL−1 GOQDs aqueous solution (15 mL) for 5 h. After slow evaporation at 40 °C in vacuum oven, the brown GOQD/TiO2-NA was produced. Then, the black GQD/TiO2-NA was prepared through annealing treatment on GOQD/TiO2-NA at 450 °C in argon stream for 1 h. Finally, the sample was washed with water before further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQD and CdS loaded TiO2 nanotubes array (GQD/CdS/TiO2-NA)
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation procedure was in accordance with the sample GQD/TiO2-NA described above except that the TiO2-NA was replaced by CdS/TiO2-NA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of graphene loaded TiO2 nanotubes array (GE/TiO2-NA)
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, the TiO2-NA was immersed in 5 mL GO (0.01 mg mL−1) aqueous solution. Then, through slow evaporation at 40 °C in a vacuum oven the dried GO/TiO2-NA sample was obtained. Finally, by directly annealing treatment on GO/TiO2-NA at 450 °C in argon stream for 1 h the GE/TiO2-NA was prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of graphene and CdS loaded TiO2 nanotubes array (GE/CdS/TiO2-NA)
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation procedure was in accordance with the sample GE/TiO2-NA described above except that the TiO2-NA was replaced by CdS/TiO2-NA.

The morphology and microstructure of the samples were characterized by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, S-4800, Hitachi) and transmission electron microscopy (JEOL-2100F) equipped with the Thermo Scientific energy-dispersive X-ray fluorescence analyzer. The X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) of the products were recorded with Bruker D8 Focus Diffraction System using Cu Kα as radiation source (λ = 0.15418 nm). Raman spectra of the samples GOQD and GQD were recorded on a DXR Microscope with 532 nm laser excitation. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on a PHI-1600 ESCA spectrometer using Mg Kα as radiation source (1253.6 eV). UV/Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded by means of a Lambda 750S (Perkin–Elmer) UV–Vis spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere. Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) was conducted using a Pyris Diamond TG/DTA analyzer (Perkin–Elmer). The sample used for TG measurement was obtained through scraping film from the Ti substrate. The sample was pretreated in nitrogen atmosphere and then transferred into air. Room-temperature photoluminescence investigation was executed on the Fluorolog3 photoluminescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, Japan).

Photocatalytic H2 evolution reaction was performed in a closed gas-circulating system with a three-electrode cell. The sheet-based samples were used as working electrodes, Ag/AgCl was used as the reference electrode and Pt foil was used as the counter electrode. They are immersed in 400 mL aqueous solution containing 0.1 M Na2S and 0.1 M Na2SO3 as sacrificial reagents. A copper wire was attached on the backside of the titanium foil using conductive paste. The exposed titanium foil and copper wire were insulated by epoxy resin. The applied positive bias (+0.6 V) was supplied by CHI electrochemical analyzer (CHI 660A Instruments). The pressure of the reaction system was kept at 1 atm, and the temperature was maintained at 60–70 °C by a flow of cooling water. Before illumination, the solution was de-aerated with nitrogen gas for 30 min to remove the dissolved oxygen. A 300 W Xenon lamp was used as the light source to simulate sunlight. The working electrodes have an active area of 1.8 cm2. The content of hydrogen was determined every 0.5 h using a gas chromatograph. After 3 h irradiation for one circle, the reaction was interrupted and the electrolyte was replaced by fresh one. The gaseous H2 evolved was periodically analyzed by an on-line Agilent 4890D gas chromatograph equipped with a TCD detector.

Fig. 1 a and b display the TEM image of Graphene Oxide Quantum Dots (GOQDs) and their size distributions. Herein, the sample obtained through solvothermal treatment on GO solution is named as GOQDs, different from previous reports [20] (The difference between GOQDs and GQDs in this work is discussed in detail below). The as-prepared GOQDs show relatively narrow size distribution with an average diameter of 3.8 nm (Fig. 1b). From Fig. 1c we could observe obvious excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behaviors for GOQDs. The PL spectra show a strong peak at 525 nm accompanied with a shoulder at 430 nm when excited at 320 nm. With gradual red shifts of the excitation wavelength from 320 to 550 nm, the main peak at 525 nm changes in a volcano-type trend, and the shoulder peak at 430 nm vanishes gradually. The origin of excitation-dependent PL behaviors for GOQDs could be attributed to the combined action of quantum size effect and surface defects [20]. The TEM image and PL spectra conformably verify that GOQDs have been successfully synthesized through a facile solvothermal reaction.

Self-organized TiO2-NA was prepared by a two-step anodic oxidation. The SEM image of the TiO2-NA as shown in Fig. 2 a indicates that the TiO2-NA is vertically oriented with a regularly arranged pore structure. From the inset in Fig. 2a, it can be seen that these tubes exhibit hexagonal structure with a diameter of approximate 150 nm and a wall thickness of ∼25 nm. A side view of the TiO2-NA (Fig. 2b) shows that all walls are entirely smooth with length up to 15 μm. The bottom image of the TiO2-NA (the inset in Fig. 2b) shows that these nanotube arrays possess closely ended hexagonal structure. The XRD patterns (Fig. S1) confirm that the TiO2-NA exists as anatase phase. The wide diffraction peak of CdS (JCPDS 80-0019) for the sample CdS/TiO2-NA could also be observed, indicating the small size and weak crystalline for CdS nanoparticles. The average crystalline size of anatase is about 10 nm when calculated using Scherrer equation on the line broadening of (101) plane. The sample CdS/TiO2-NA was also investigated by TEM and matched EDX (Fig. 2c and d). The TEM image (Fig. 2c) of the CdS/TiO2-NA sample shows that the ordered tubular structure is well remained after CdS deposition; and many CdS nanoparticles have been loaded into the pore of TiO2 nanotubes. The deposited particles are CdS quantum dots consisting of Cd and S elements as confirmed by the EDX analysis (inset in Fig. 2c). In HR-TEM image (Fig. 2d), the lattice spacing of the (220) plane of CdS (∼0.21 nm) and that of the (110) plane of anatase (∼0.27 nm) are clearly observed, which are consistent with the XRD observations. The histogram in Fig. 2e confirms that the CdS quantum dots have an average diameter of 9.1 nm. Above results indicate that the CdS nanoparticles have been successfully deposited into the as-prepared TiO2-NA by sequential chemical bath deposition, and the deposition process has not damaged the ordered TiO2-NA structure at all (Fig. S2).

To confirm the formation of GQDs by annealing treatment of GOQDs in argon at 450 °C, the samples were examined by PL spectroscopy and UV/Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 3 . It can be seen that the thermal annealing process induces the disappearance of fluorescent signal, which is the intrinsic property of GOQDs [20]. Moreover, the UV/Vis absorption ability for GQDs increases a lot after annealing treatment, which is consistent with the color change from brown to black after calcination. So, it is concluded that the GQDs have been obtained by the annealing reduction of GOQDs, which is also supported by the results of Raman spectroscopy as shown in Fig. S3.

The diffuse reflectance UV/Vis absorption spectra of as-prepared inversely structured catalysts are shown in Fig. 4 . The edge of the characteristic absorption band for TiO2-NA could be distinguished at the wavelength ∼385 nm by extrapolating the steep slopes in the curve to the long wavelength side. For sample CdS/TiO2-NA, an obvious red shift of absorption band edge to visible range is found owing to the narrower band gap of CdS quantum dots. The absorption intensity gets stronger after loading of GQDs for both TiO2-NA and CdS/TiO2-NA catalysts, especially in the visible light range, which is due to the entire spectral absorption by graphene (Fig. 3) [10–12]. The results of UV/Vis absorption confirm that the anchoring of GQDs into TiO2-NA and CdS/TiO2-NA could increase the photon absorptive capacity of the samples and maintain the intrinsic band gap of semiconductors.

Fig. 5 shows the C 1s binding energy spectra from XPS for the samples GOQD/CdS/TiO2-NA and GQD/CdS/TiO2-NA. It is found that there are two binding energy peaks in each spectrum, corresponding to two kinds of carbon species. The peak at 284.7 eV corresponds to the carbon with sp2 electronic configuration. The other peak at higher binding energy (288.1 eV) is often attributed to oxygenated carbon species such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and epoxide species [12,23]. Since the C 1s peaks in Fig. 5 are not symmetric, they are deconvoluted and simulated by a standard Gaussian–Lorentzian to get a relative content of different carbon species. The deconvolution results show that the percentage of oxygenated carbon species (288.1 eV) is 31% and 16% for sample GOQD/CdS/TiO2-NA and GQD/CdS/TiO2-NA, respectively [24]. The decrease of the oxygenated carbon species for the sample GQD/CdS/TiO2-NA clearly indicates that the GOQDs have been reduced at least in part to GQDs through annealing treatment. It should be noted that the peak of Ti–C bonds around 281 eV is not found for both the samples GOQD/CdS/TiO2-NA and GQD/CdS/TiO2-NA, implying that carbon atom does not enter into the lattice of TiO2 [25]. The Ti 2p XPS spectrum of GQD/CdS/TiO2-NA contains two peaks at 458.5 eV and 464.2 eV (Fig. S4), which are attributed to anatase TiO2 [23]. Besides, the annealing treatment could strengthen the interaction between GQDs and support TiO2 so that the GQDs can be fixed into the nanotubes to increase the stability of the catalysts. This is of great importance for photocatalytic hydrogen generation in solutions because the GOQDs can dissolve into aqueous solutions owing to the existence of abundant surface oxygenated functional groups.

EDX and thermogravimetry (TG) tests were used for quantitative analysis on the composition (Fig. 6 and Fig. S5). For sample CdS/TiO2-NA, EDX results (Fig. 6a) clearly show that the molar ratio of Cd/Ti is about 0.95/10, which is close to the value for the sample GQD/CdS/TiO2-NA (Fig. S5). This indicates that the loading of GQODs and subsequent thermal treatment have not changed the relative compositions between CdS and TiO2. The content of GQDs in GQD/TiO2-NA is determined by TG analysis in Fig. 6b. It is clear that about 0.5 wt% GQDs exists in GQD/TiO2-NA.

Fig. 7 shows the photocatalytic activity on hydrogen production by water splitting over the samples TiO2-NA, GQD/TiO2-NA, CdS/TiO2-NA and GQD/CdS/TiO2-NA. The content of hydrogen was determined every 0.5 h using a gas chromatograph. It can be seen that the amount of produced hydrogen exhibits a linear relationship with radiation time in every circle of 3 h, indicating a permanent stability of the monolithic photoanodes. The photocatalytic activity order for different catalysts is: GQD/CdS/TiO2-NA > CdS/TiO2-NA > GQD/TiO2-NA > TiO2-NA. The anchored GQDs did improve the photocatalytic hydrogen production activity for both TiO2-NA and CdS/TiO2-NA photoanodes. The presence of CdS further increases the activity of the catalyst due to its good response to visible light. Notably, the positive effect of GQDs on CdS/TiO2-NA is much bigger than that on TiO2-NA. It may arise from the suppression effect on the charge carriers recombination and fast electron transport through the GQDs. The above results demonstrate that the as-designed photoanodes with GQDs could increase the light quantum efficiency especially for the composite materials.

For a comparative research on the effect of filtering light by graphene, the samples GE/TiO2-NA and GE/CdS/TiO2-NA with the loading of conventional graphene sheets were synthesized with the same content of graphene (Fig. S6). We investigated the hydrogen generation rate over all the photoanodes under different incident light intensity through adding neutral attenuation piece. As seen in Fig. 8 , the activity for all the catalysts is reduced with the decrease of light intensity. It can also be found that the loading of GE exhibits a negative effect on hydrogen generation for monolithic photoanodes and such effect increases with the decrease of incident light intensity. This could be ascribed to the stronger light absorption of large graphene sheets [13]. In comparison, the enhancement effect of GQDs on hydrogen generation rate is well maintained in the case of low light intensity. These results further confirm the viability of weakening light absorption during photocatalytic process by breaking graphene sheet into GQDs.

In summary, the inversely structured photocatalysts with GQDs anchored into TiO2-NA and CdS/TiO2-NA were designed and successfully synthesized. The thermal reduction in argon is demonstrated to be essential for not only transforming GOQDs into GQDs but also fixing GQDs onto the semiconductors supports. The obviously increased hydrogen generation activity of the GQDs-modified catalysts could be attributed to the promotional effect of GQDs on charge separation and transfer. The light-filtering effect of large graphene sheets can be remarkably weakened by breaking graphene into GQDs. Moreover, the intrinsic morphology of TiO2-NA gets well maintained after loading GQDs. Such inversely structured design of photocatalysts greatly increases the light quantum efficiency and the photocatalytic activity for hydrogen production. So, it could be referenced for the design of other composite photocatalytic systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21076146, 21276184), the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China (No.20120032110014), the Program of New Century Excellent Talents in University of China (No. NCET–07–0599). The authors are also grateful to the Program of Introducing Talents of Discipline to University of China (No. B06006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2013.07.039.
